title: Starter run
question: What does the line 'drop_ball()' do in the demo program?
boardHint: The first run is only a demo. One ball enters the board through the current input.
unlockConnection: You watch one run first, then clear two quick checks before the editor unlocks.
options: It launches one ball through the current input.
It launches one ball through input 1.
It skips the next visible ball.
It changes the chosen input before the launch.
successMessage: Correct. It launches one ball from the default input.
failureMessage: The starter line only launches one ball. It does not skip, reroute, or open anything.

title: Input numbers
question: Which input number is the center input?
boardHint: The same numbers appear later in 'choose_input(...)'.
unlockConnection: Knowing the input numbers makes the first editable programs easier to read.
options: There is no center input.
successMessage: Correct. Input 2 is the center input.
failureMessage: The board uses 1 = Left, 2 = Center, 3 = Right.

title: Trace 'portal_side'
question: If the assignment 'target = portal_side' runs while 'portal_side' is '3', which input does this program use?
boardHint: The portal label shows both the side name and the code value.
unlockConnection: An assignment can copy the visible 'portal_side' value into your own variable so you can reuse it for input choice.
options: Input 1
Input 2
Input 3
No ball launches, because 'target' is only a variable name.
successMessage: Correct. 'target' becomes '3', so the ball launches from the right input.
failureMessage: The assignment makes 'target' copy 'portal_side'. If 'portal_side' is '3', the program chooses the right input.

title: Repair the input value
question: Which assignment replacement fixes the program so it follows the portal safely?
boardHint: The assignment should copy 'portal_side' directly, without adding to it.
unlockConnection: This checks whether you understand that the assignment should reuse 'portal_side' directly.
options: target = portal_side
target = portal_side + 1
target = 4 - portal_side
target = 2
successMessage: Correct. 'portal_side' already has the input value, so the program can use it directly.
failureMessage: 'portal_side' already stores the active side. Adding to it in the assignment can push the input choice out of range.

title: Write one opposite-side launch
question: Write one short program that works for every visible case below: use an assignment to store 'portal_side' in your own variable, then launch exactly one ball through the opposite side.
boardHint: Start with an assignment such as 'target = portal_side'. Then use '4 - target' to switch left <-> right.
unlockConnection: This checks whether you can write an assignment first and then use the stored value inside an expression.

cases.title: Portal left
cases.requirement: Store 'portal_side' in your own variable, then launch exactly one ball through the right side.

cases.title: Portal right
cases.requirement: Store 'portal_side' in your own variable, then launch exactly one ball through the left side.

successMessage: Correct. That code stores the visible value and uses it to flip to the opposite side.
failureMessage: Your answer should use an assignment to store 'portal_side' in your own variable and launch exactly one ball through the opposite side.

title: Repair the preview rule
question: Which version skips only 'negative_ball' and still launches the others?
boardHint: The fix should compare 'next_ball' to 'negative_ball', then keep a real launch for the safe balls.
unlockConnection: Conditionals matter because the machine can react to the preview before the ball enters the board.
successMessage: Correct. The repaired version skips only 'negative_ball' and still launches the others.
failureMessage: The fix must compare 'next_ball' to 'negative_ball', skip that case, and still launch the other balls.

title: Repair the full preview strategy
question: Which program correctly handles the preview: skip 'negative_ball', send 'portal_ball' to the portal, and send 'center_ball' to input 2?
boardHint: The repaired program should keep 3 outcomes distinct: negative -> skip, portal -> portal_side, center -> input 2.
unlockConnection: This is the first full preview strategy, but here you only need to recognize the correct branching logic instead of writing it from scratch.
successMessage: Correct. That version keeps the three visible cases separate and routes each one correctly.
failureMessage: The correct version should skip only 'negative_ball', send 'portal_ball' to 'portal_side', and send 'center_ball' to input 2.

title: Review: read the portal
question: If 'portal_side' is '1', where does this program launch the ball?
boardHint: Earlier topics still matter. Preview logic only helps if you still read the portal correctly.
unlockConnection: This is a spaced review of the variables topic.
options: Input 1, because 'portal_side' is passed straight into 'choose_input(...)'.
Input 2, because the center input is the default launch lane.
Input 3, because 'portal_side' should be mirrored to the opposite side.
No ball launches, because there is no 'if' statement.
failureMessage: When 'portal_side' is '1', the ball should launch from the left input.

title: Trace the helper call
question: What happens when the main program calls 'follow_portal()'?
boardHint: The helper editor stores one tactic, and the main editor can call it by name.
unlockConnection: Functions matter because one named tactic can control repeated board behavior.
options: The helper body runs once at the call site.
Only the helper definition is stored, so no ball launches.
The helper body keeps repeating until the preview is empty.
The helper changes 'portal_side' before it launches.
successMessage: Correct. Calling 'follow_portal()' runs that helper body once.
failureMessage: A helper call executes the helper body once at the call site.

title: Write 'follow_portal()'
question: Write 'follow_portal()' so it chooses the input above the portal and launches one ball.
boardHint: This helper should read 'portal_side', use it inside 'choose_input(...)', and then call 'drop_ball()'.
unlockConnection: This checkpoint checks whether you can write your own portal-following helper instead of only calling one that was already given to you.

cases.title: Portal left
cases.requirement: When the portal is left, 'follow_portal()' should launch exactly one ball from input 1.

cases.title: Portal right
cases.requirement: When the portal is right, 'follow_portal()' should launch exactly one ball from input 3.

successMessage: Correct. Your helper reads 'portal_side' and launches once from the matching input.
failureMessage: Your helper should define 'follow_portal()', choose the input from 'portal_side', and launch exactly one ball.

title: Review: repair the negative-ball check
question: Which condition skips only 'negative_ball'?
boardHint: Earlier branch logic still matters when you move tactics into helpers later.
unlockConnection: This is a spaced review of the conditions topic.
options: next_ball == negative_ball
next_ball == portal_ball
next_ball != negative_ball
portal_side == 1
successMessage: Correct. 'next_ball == negative_ball' skips only the negative ball.
failureMessage: The condition should read 'next_ball' and compare it directly to 'negative_ball'.

title: Repair the loop body
question: Which version skips a 'negative_ball' and continues safely with the loop?
boardHint: After 'skip_ball()', the current loop step should stop before it reaches 'follow_portal()'.
unlockConnection: This is the key loop pattern that turns one tactic into safe automation.
successMessage: Correct. 'continue' stops that loop step after the negative ball is skipped.
failureMessage: The safe version skips the negative ball and uses 'continue' so the loop does not launch it afterward.

title: Write the repeated preview loop
question: Write one loop that handles the 4 upcoming balls shown in the preview: send 'portal_ball' to the portal directly, skip 'negative_ball', and send every 'center_ball' to input 2.
boardHint: This checkpoint removes the helper. One loop should still work for both visible queues. 'elif' is a good fit here, and 'continue' is optional, but the negative-ball branch must not fall through into a launch. Extra repetitions are okay as long as the visible preview is handled correctly.
unlockConnection: This checks real transfer: you now have to write the full portal / negative / center strategy directly inside the loop.

cases.title: Portal, negative, center, portal
cases.requirement: Send each 'portal_ball' to the portal directly, skip 'negative_ball', send 'center_ball' to input 2, and still handle the final 'portal_ball' correctly.

cases.title: Negative, center, center, portal
cases.requirement: Skip 'negative_ball', send both 'center_ball's to input 2, and send the final 'portal_ball' to the portal directly.

successMessage: Correct. Your loop handles the longer mixed preview directly, without leaning on a helper call.
failureMessage: Your loop should skip 'negative_ball', send every 'center_ball' to input 2, and send every 'portal_ball' to the portal directly.

title: Review: call the helper twice
question: How many helper launches does this main program trigger?
boardHint: A function call runs the stored helper body each time it appears.
unlockConnection: This is a spaced review of the functions topic.
successMessage: Correct. Two calls trigger two helper launches.
failureMessage: Each 'follow_portal()' call runs the helper body once, so two calls mean two helper launches.

title: Trace the bonus map
question: If 'bonus_map = [0.5, 1, 5]', what multiplier does 'bonus_map[2]' read?
boardHint: Index 0 is the first item, so index 2 is the third multiplier, which belongs to input 3.
unlockConnection: This checks whether you can read one input multiplier directly out of 'bonus_map'.
options: Index 2 is outside the list.
failureMessage: Remember that list indexing starts at 0, so index 2 is the third item.

title: Repair the best-input tracker
question: Which version checks every multiplier, remembers the strongest input, and launches that input?
code:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
choose_input(best_index)
drop_ball()
boardHint: Walk through 'bonus_map' directly. Keep the best multiplier and the matching zero-based index, then add 1 before 'choose_input(...)'.
unlockConnection: Index access and direct iteration work together here: read each multiplier, remember the best one, and convert its index into input 1, 2, or 3.
options:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
for multiplier in range(3):
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = multiplier
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_index = multiplier
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = 0
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()
successMessage: Correct. The repaired version loops directly over 'bonus_map', keeps the best multiplier, and launches the matching lane.
failureMessage: The fixed version should walk 'bonus_map', remember the strongest multiplier and its index, and then choose that input.

title: Write the best-input farmer
question: Write one program that works for every visible case below: use 'bonus_map' to find the strongest input, then launch at least 3 balls from that input.
boardHint: Start with 'best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]' and 'best_index = 0'. Walk through 'bonus_map', remember the best index, then use a second loop to fire at least 3 times from 'best_index + 1'. 'range(3)' is the smallest loop that works here.
unlockConnection: This is the lists capstone: search the input-multiplier list, keep the best index, and then exploit that input repeatedly.

cases.title: Strongest in the middle
cases.requirement: Find the strongest multiplier and launch at least 3 balls from the middle input when the bonus map is [0.5, 5, 1].

cases.title: Strongest on the left
cases.requirement: Find the strongest multiplier and launch at least 3 balls from the left input when the bonus map is [5, 1, 0.5].

cases.title: Strongest on the right
cases.requirement: Find the strongest multiplier and launch at least 3 balls from the right input when the bonus map is [1, 0.5, 5].

successMessage: Correct. Your program finds the strongest input, remembers its index, and farms that input for at least 3 launches.
failureMessage: Your answer should walk 'bonus_map', keep the strongest multiplier and index, and then launch at least 3 balls from the matching input.
